content: Aortic stenosis (AS) is obstruction of left ventricular outflow due to a narrowed aortic valve. Etiologies: degenerative calcific (most common in elderly—age-related, similar pathobiology to atherosclerosis), congenital bicuspid valve (presents 50s-60s vs 70s-80s for tricuspid), rheumatic heart disease (rare in developed countries, associated with mitral disease). Pathophysiology: progressive valve narrowing increases LV afterload, causing concentric hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, eventually systolic dysfunction and heart failure. Classic triad: angina (increased demand, decreased supply), syncope (fixed output unable to increase with exertion), dyspnea/heart failure (elevated filling pressures). Once symptoms develop, average survival without intervention: angina 5 years, syncope 3 years, heart failure 2 years. Severity classification: mild (jet velocity 2.0-2.9 m/s, mean gradient <20 mmHg, AVA >1.5 cm²), moderate (3.0-3.9 m/s, 20-39 mmHg, 1.0-1.5 cm²), severe (≥4.0 m/s, ≥40 mmHg, ≤1.0 cm²), very severe (≥5.0 m/s, ≥60 mmHg). Low-flow low-gradient AS requires dobutamine stress echo or CT calcium scoring.
